# Transcrição: 05-'Why do you do the things you do.'

- **Fonte:** 05-'Why do you do the things you do.'
- **Duração:** 05:40
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

The question I like to ask myself, the one that helps me find the most stories, the one that helps me find the most understanding about myself is this. Why do you do the things that you do? When I ask myself that question about things that I do in my everyday life, I often find stories. The goal is to look at your life, to look at the things that you do, the things that don't quite make sense, the things you've never told anyone about before, the things you might not dare to tell anyone about. Those are the things you want to ask this question about. So for example, last year I was in my classroom, my students were at recess, and a fly was buzzing around the classroom. And I looked at the fly and I said, go live your best life fly. And it occurred to me, I'm constantly telling flies to live their best lives. And because I love that question, why do you do the things that you do? And I suddenly realized, this is a weird thing that I do that I've never told anyone about before. I stood in the middle of my room and said, why do you do the things that you do? Why do you tell flies to live their best life? And as soon as I asked the question, I knew immediately why I do it. I live in a constant, perilous, endless existential crisis. I think about my death all the time and I'm constantly worried about it. I suddenly understood in my classroom that when I see a fly buzzing around the room, I'm keenly aware that they live for about three days. Every time I see a fly, I see it at the end of its life. And I'm worried that it's not living its best life. I'm looking at the end of my life through a fly and worried that at the end of my life, I will look back and be concerned that I have not lived my best life. You can see the understanding that comes with asking that question. You can see how that might easily become a story, a story that relates flies to a man's existential crisis. Those two things can be combined together to form a great story. Let me give you one more. Two years ago, I was playing golf with my friend Steve. It was 102 degrees on this day and I had left my Gatorade in the car. We were on the sixth hole climbing a hill and Steve heard me huffing and puffing and he said, Hey, I have an extra Gatorade. Do you want mine? And I said no, which was crazy because I was dying and it was 102 degrees. But I said no and suffered for the last three holes of the round before I finally got to my car. Steve drove away, but I was sitting behind my wheel in the air conditioning and I thought back on saying no to that Gatorade. And I asked myself, why do you do the things that you do?

## [03:03 - 05:40]

Why do you always say no when people offer you food and drink? And as soon as I asked the question, I knew the answer. When I was a kid, I grew up hungry. There was never enough food in the house. And what you discover as a hungry child is it's better to be hungry than to take handouts from other people because if someone offers you food and you take it, you know that you can never reciprocate. So it's better to be hungry and quiet about it than to be ashamed and never be able to return a favor. I was a 48 year old man climbing a hill on 102 degree day, but I was also a 10 year old boy. That 10 year old boy who was still worried about food and drink, even though I have a refrigerator full of all that I need, that 10 year old boy was still trapped in me and I was reacting to the world like a 10 year old boy. You can imagine how I felt sitting in that car that day. Having understanding that I'd spent my entire life refusing the offer of food because somewhere buried inside me, I was still a boy hungry and ashamed of it. When you ask yourself the question, why do I do the things that I do? You'll find enormous personal understanding. But more importantly, you'll find some of the most important stories you have to tell because they'll be the stories that you have to tell yourself first. The stories that you've been waiting to tell yourself for all your life. The stories that have been sitting right in front of you and you didn't see them because you didn't take time to just think about you. So lock your children in the basement. Send your partner out for a coffee. Tell everyone in the house to evacuate. 10 minutes every day. Sit down and think about yourself. Only yourself. And when you find yourself moving through the world, doing something that's a little odd, a little different, a little unmentionable, pause, take a moment and ask yourself, why do I do the things that I do? I promise you the answers are stories. I promise you that those stories are for yourself first and then likely the world is going to love them too. Ask yourself that question. Why do you do the things that you do?
